title: Hand-sew a running stitch and a backstitch
subtitle: The two foundation hand-sewing stitches every other hand seam builds on.
excerpt: Running stitch is fast, light, and used for gathering, basting, and visible-mending sashiko. Backstitch is the strong one: it holds like a machine seam and is the right choice for any load-bearing hand seam. One short practice piece teaches both.
sourceNotes:
Method drawn from two public-domain references:

• Beeton's Book of Needlework (1870), opening chapter on plain stitching. Project Gutenberg #25640.
• Caulfeild & Saward, The Dictionary of Needlework (1882), entries for Running and Backstitch. Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: A line of long, loose temporary stitches that holds fabric pieces in place while the proper seam is sewn. Removed afterwards.
The strip of fabric between the seam line and the cut edge of the fabric. The standard hand-sewn seam allowance is 1 to 1.5 cm; the strip stays on the inside of the finished piece.

content.text: Running stitch and backstitch are the two foundation stitches of hand sewing. Both are worked with a single thread on a single needle, on the same piece of fabric, in the same direction. The difference is the path the needle takes. Running stitch is a simple in-and-out: fast, light, and good for gathering, basting, and decorative sashiko. Backstitch goes one step forward and half a step back, like a tiny zigzag from the side, forming a continuous unbroken line on the front of the fabric. It's the hand-sewn equivalent of a machine stitch and the right choice for any seam that has to hold.

Twenty minutes with a scrap of cotton, a needle, and a length of contrasting thread teaches both. The two stitches together cover almost every hand-sewing need a household has: hemming, mending, attaching buttons, basting before machine-sewing, repairing a child's torn knee.
What you need

A scrap of cotton or linen fabric the size of a postcard. A sharp hand-sewing needle (size 7 or 8 for general cotton work). About 50 cm of all-purpose polyester thread in a colour that contrasts with the fabric, so the stitches show clearly. Scissors. A thimble is helpful but not essential at the practice stage.
Threading the needle and knotting the end

Cut a 50 cm length of thread (longer than that and it tangles). Pass one end through the needle's eye and pull about 15 cm through; the working stitches will use the long single strand, not a doubled-over loop. Knot the cut end at the long side.

The standard knot is a small loop tied around the end of the thread: lay the cut end against the index finger, wrap the long thread once around the fingertip, then roll the wrap off the finger between thumb and finger until it tightens into a small ball. Repeat once if the knot looks slack.
The running stitch

A running stitch is the simplest possible stitch: needle up through the fabric, down again a short distance ahead, up again the same distance after that. From the front you see a row of short dashes with equal gaps. From the back you see the same row offset by one stitch.
Push the needle up through the fabric from the back. The knot stops the thread pulling all the way through.
Push the needle back down through the fabric about 3 mm ahead.
Push the needle up again about 3 mm past where it went down. The fabric is gathering on the needle as you go.
After four or five stitches, pull the needle all the way through to set the row. Carry on along the line.
Keep the stitch length even (the down-and-up cycle the same) and the spacing between stitches the same. The line should read as a row of dashes, not a scribble.
Use a running stitch for:
basting

(with longer 1 cm stitches), gathering (with several rows pulled at once to draw the fabric up), visible-mending sashiko (with denser 2 mm stitches and contrasting thread), and any decorative line where strength doesn't matter.
The backstitch

A backstitch is the same in-and-out motion but the needle goes backwards each time before going forward, leaving an unbroken line on the front of the fabric. It looks identical to a machine stitch from the front.
Push the needle up through the fabric from the back to the front.
Push the needle down 3 mm back behind where it came up (going backwards into the work you've already done, or for the first stitch, into a point 3 mm back from the start).
Push the needle up again 3 mm forward of where it last came up (so 6 mm forward of where it just went down).
Push the needle down 3 mm back into the front of the previous stitch's end-point. The new down-point and the previous up-point meet exactly.
Repeat. Each stitch goes back to meet the end of the previous stitch, then forward by twice that distance to the next up-point.
From the front the line is unbroken. From the back the thread overlaps itself, forming a doubled-over line that's tougher than the front. This is what gives the backstitch its strength.
Use a backstitch for: any load-bearing hand seam (a torn knee, a ripped pocket, a re-attached button shank), a hand-sewn seam where a machine isn't to hand, and any
seam allowance
that needs to hold under tension.
Ending the thread

When the thread runs low (about 10 cm left), end on the back of the fabric. Make a small loop in the thread, pass the needle through it twice to form a knot tight against the fabric, then pass the needle once more through a few stitches on the back to bury the tail. Trim flush.
Common mistakes

attrs.heading: What goes wrong on a first practice piece
attrs.intro: Four habits that show up on a first hand-sewn line.

items.symptom: Stitches are uneven, some long and some short
items.cause: The hand is moving faster than the eye can track each stitch's length.
items.fix: Slow down. Pause at each stitch and check the next down-point is the same distance ahead as the last one. Speed comes once the eye and hand are calibrated, which is usually after the first 50 stitches.

items.symptom: The fabric puckers along the line of stitching
items.cause: Thread tension is too tight on each stitch; the thread is pulling the fabric in.
items.fix: Pull each stitch firm against the fabric, not tight. The line should lie flat against the cloth. Practise on a hoop or on fabric pinned to a table for the first dozen stitches.

items.symptom: The thread keeps tangling and knotting on itself
items.cause: The thread is twisting as the needle goes through the fabric, building up tension that eventually loops back on itself.
items.fix: Let the needle dangle every six or eight stitches so the thread untwists. If the thread is fluffy or coated (some polyester threads are), beeswax or a thread conditioner helps.

items.symptom: Backstitch line shows a wavy front instead of an unbroken straight line
items.cause: The forward distance is varying between stitches, so the up-points don't sit on a straight line.
items.fix: Mark a faint pencil line on the fabric before starting. Take each up-point exactly on the line. After the first row of practice, the eye learns to work without the line.

content.text: Where this goes next

Running stitch and backstitch together cover most household hand-sewing. The natural next set is hemming stitches (slipstitch and whipstitch) for finishing a folded hem invisibly; the buttonhole stitch for a hand-bound buttonhole; and the whip stitch for closing the gap on a turned-out cushion or soft toy. All of them build on the in-and-out motion of these two.
